quotes.quote: I find when I try and swing too hard things go wrong!
Quit trying to kill it.
So many amateur golfers don't understand the difference between a hard swing, bad timing, proper tempo, and other factors.
A problem almost every golfer encounters is rushing the downswing - letting loose with everything you have the moment the backswing is complete.
The shoulders outrace the arms, the arms outrace the hands, and the hands outrace the club. All that speed is expended too soon.
Let's face it, everyone wants extra distance.
Thanks to Bryson's incredible transformation in 2020, the golf world is all about power and distance.
Swinging harder isn't always the solution to added distance though. Yes, speed does create distance. But you need to make sure you have optimal speed at the right time.
Without knowing these numbers, it's very easy to swing too hard and hit some bad shots. This is why you need to practice and track your carry distances.
The easiest way to do this is with a portable launch monitor. Luckily, they're cheaper than ever, can fit in your pocket, and tell you more about your swing than you ever thought possible.
One great way to check your tempo is with the deWiz golf watch. This incredible training aid makes it easy to see if you rush your backswing, transition, or downswing.
If you need help feeling a faster backswing, download the Tour Tempo app on your phone.
SuperSpeed sticks can help you start swinging faster but still maintain proper tempo to hit the ball further than ever.

I love SuperSpeed sticks and we've written extensively about them... because they are so effective. While they're not an overnight fix to more distance, they're a great tool to help you increase swing speed over time.
But I've found that if you use them right before a round, they might accidentally lead to swinging too hard.

One is too easy of a swing off the tee box and the other is trying to 'kill it' swing. Yes, sometimes I can kill it, but the majority of the time the 'kill it' swing turns into a lost shot because I am going to banana it or top it.
I honestly have no idea. I think I swing easy, but I have absolutely no clue if i'm swing too hard to too little still to this day and how I would even begin to quantify the % haha.
I'd venture a guess and say it was 90% or so. Anything beyond that and I slice the heck out of the ball.
I think this is why I hit my 4 wood so good. I simply swing it smoother and about 90% of capacity.
I know I absolutely have better control when my swing is conserved some. I just don't have the control at what I consider a 100% swing.
I'm mostly in the 80-85% range. I'll sometimes go more than that with a driver which is maybe why I'm so much straighter with my 3 wood.
As I've aged I've slowly come to the realization that I don't have that 6th gear anymore.
I would say full out. My youngest son was at a PGA tourney and videod Zalatoris (SP?) and sent it to me. The dude's swing looked so violent it was like his spine was going to crack wide open.
I used to be 100% every shot and would look at a yardage and pick my max out distance club. I now swing about 90 percent and take one club less and or choke down.

As for Bryson, I saw him in person at the northern trust. His swing with driver is comparable to that of a new golfer trying to hit it over the fence. Just so violent and I wonder how long his body can maintain that much force.
I have no idea what my % is. I do know where my red line is and I work hard on not exceeding it. I get great contact and my best distance when I slow things down just enough.
I live in the 75-90% range. Call it 80 on average. It's very controllable and repeatable for me there.
Their swings are so smooth because they're probably only swinging at 85-90% max. That's part of the reason their ball striking is so good.
I've found that my faster swings don't give me that much more 'useable' distance. Most of the time those 100%+ swings turn into ball searching, rescue missions.
The key for me is to ensure that I am still in balance. If I can finish in balance I can try to add more speed. If I can't I need to throttle it back.
I swing as hard as I can still maintain tempo and balance on full swings - what percent that is I have no idea, but there's still a little bit left in the tank.
Tempo and sequencing beat swing effort for me. Hard part is getting the darn brain to believe that in the heat of the moment.
I would strongly recommend that you go get a lesson with ANY person who can look at your swing and give valuable commentary.
Just think of it as a 'strong 6 iron', and focus on making a nice SMOOTH swing rather than trying to kill it.

I was able to smash some drives as I was trying to kill it, and got away with it, 220-230 for me, but the next shot, oh well. I need to find some middle ground on hitting it hard, and not losing control.
I still open the face on my SW but swing too hard. The ball comes out and goes 41 yards through the back of the green.
This round was just frustrating. I thought I started out decent and also thought I might turn it around on the back after paring 8 and 9. Nope, not gonna happen.
I think my driver went south on me on the back because I was frustrated by the mess I made on the front 9 with short game and putting. I was not calm nor as 'smooth'.
Arccos has me at -31.8 strokes gained compared to a 0 HCP for the round.
Strength + speed training: 2-3 weekly sessions of short, explosive swings with an overspeed tool (SuperSpeed) or light drivers.
Work on a repeatable swing plane and release pattern: alignment sticks on the ground and two-ball drill.
I thought the same thing myself until a lesson with a PGA Tour coach changed my perspective. I was always taught 'Low and slow' on the way back.
In reality, swinging slower was actually making things worse.
If you're feeling quick during transition, speed up your backswing.
Put all the gear and gadgets away, simply swing at 50%. Then, work your way to up to 70% speed.
A lot of people think to hit the ball hard, they need a firm grip on the club. But in reality, too much grip pressure can actually lead to big misses and a loss of distance.

Unfortunately I don't think I will ever get to a point where I can say if I hit this 84% power the ball will fly x distance. Just don't have that much time on my hands to practice on the range to be that skilled.

Back in my younger days when I was playing my best golf, 80% was the norm for me. Now did I know it was 80%? Not hardly. It was just the fastest I could swing, and still have my correct tempo, timing, in balanced swing.
My 100% speed swing feels like 75% effort. My 100% swing ends up like 75% swing speed.
I go with 90% and if I think 80% it becomes too slow and a bad swing with lack of power and follow through. It's a fine line.
With irons, it's always 100%, which I define as 'as hard as I can swing and still keep my balance.'
I probably swing harder than needed. With driver I know it is smooth and only 80% range. Fairways when I try to swing harder it gets me in trouble so smooth is king with those.
The only times I'll swing absolutely full out are in scrambles and sometimes on range practice sessions - reason being I can push my ceiling of 'in tempo' and 'in control' up a little higher.
I also learned early on that not every golfer's 80%, in control swing, was the same speed.
Now since I have increased my swing speed I am hitting that club easy 125+ most of the time which has caused me some new challenges on my distance.
The only solution for me was to work on my swing and improve the quality of contact and learn to control the slice.
Hitting a 9 iron 150 yards is pretty much an achievement and about the distance the average tour player would get out of it.
Inconsistent strike location (heel/toe or fat/thin shots reduce ball speed).
Low clubhead speed or inefficient transfer of energy (poor sequencing).
Most amateur players gain 5-20 yards per iron after: proper impact position, small increases in clubhead speed, and correcting loft/flex mismatches.
